speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Lewis Messersmith and Hazel Bramlett Messersmith took place at their home in Genesee on January 21, 1975. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I recall at the time of the Lapwai, I have a little story on, I'm just going to use his first name, Lynn, which his name was Lynn Henry, but then he used to come down to a town there and he could cross his leg and sit down and tell you more of the stories about history and things that he'd done. And he was a good storyteller. Well, after so many years, there's quite a number of us there in Lapwai.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Will you play this back to me when I tell you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you want to hear some?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Sure. A number of us there in town, we saw where there was an ad or something in the paper that they were putting on a Liars Club down in California. So a lot of us knew that Lynn Henry told a lot of good stories and we decided to kind of get together and maybe raise enough money to send him down there to this contest. And by the way, she did. He got to go down there and this was his story. I'm going to break some of it out there. This was his story. When he came back, why, he says that, yes, he says it was wonderful. But he said the last night we were there, we got in the hotel and we got to meeting the different ones that was in this contest and the Liars contest. So he said, I met a Texan. Lynn, let me ask the first question. Okay, so go ahead. So, let me ask the Texan.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He says, how do you when birds heard things?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He says down there in Texas, oh, he says, we never go out. He says anything but bull and harrow. Oh, that must be a lot of fun.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I know after so long a time when the Texan asked Lynn, he says, how do you hear so much about that hunting you do up there in Northern Idaho? And he says, how do you fellas hunt up there? And what are you use?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, Lynn says we never go hunting up there with anything else but a hunting knife.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: So, the party broke up that night and after about two years, the Texan got to thinking about this. And he thought, gee, that would be a thrill. He said to go up to Idaho. He says, what Lynn told us, he says to go on one of those hunting trips with him. So he came up and he hunted up old Lynn and sure enough, Lynn had a few mules and things and he did a little packing and so on. He knew the mountains and so on. And he says, okay, he says, we'll go up. He says on the Craig Mountains area, he says, I think you're just about right so that we can do some hunting up there. So they packed up and took a little grub along for a few days, supplies. And when they got up there, Lynn knew where there was an old cabin. That was kind of not occupied, but thought they'd go up there. When they got there, where they began to unpack the old mules and put the supplies in on the table. And finally, Lynn says, now Tex, he says you kind of straighten things up around here a little bit. And I'll go out and see if there's any signs of game here. So I went on up the trail, he says it was in the summertime and Huckleberry's was ripe. And he says, they were bugging along up through the trail and he says, all at once I blacked berries just flashed right up in front of my eyes. This is the meanest looking thing I ever saw. And Lynn, he says, I just took on down the trail as fast as I could run that bear right after me. And he was right on my heels by the time I reached the cabin door. And he says, when I run through the cabin door, he says that bear followed right in. And he says, I run around the table. And as I got out through the door, slammed the door, shut the hardware back to Tex, he says, kid, then the damn thing on. He says, I'll go out and get the other one.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Now, do you ever hear of the one my kid ordered to it?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And it was all bullshit stories. But then the next day or two, where they decided to maybe go down a little lower country and see what the game was there. It's not on now, you know. I just put it on, yeah. I just put it on.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: So, went down there and they set up camp again. And Lynn says, when they got to their camp set up, Lynn said again, well, he said they'll go up here and see what we can find up here in the way of game. And Tex says, wait a minute, Lately says I'm going right with you.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: OK?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They started up the trail and after a while, Lynn being in the lead, all at once he just stopped in the trail. And Tex, he backed right up against his belly, right against his back. And looked over and there was the biggest damn rattlesnake it was ever seen. He stood there for a while, just quiet, never saying a word.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And finally Tex said, say, Lynn, he said, are you going to do anything?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He said, just a minute. He says, I think I have. Now, if you can figure that one out. Wait a minute. That one flew by me.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Was it Lynn brought the rattlesnake on him? Lynn brought the rattlesnake out?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Didn't we get that across right?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That's the best part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Lynn do?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Lynn hesitated on the track because there was a rattlesnake right in the trail. And Tex was walking along back of him. He walked right up against his belly, right up against Tex. And they were standing there, looking at that rattlesnake, just a rattling to beat hell. That's not only that. Yeah. Go on. It sounded like hell.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And finally Tex says, Lynn, he says, are you going to do anything?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He hesitated for quite a while. He says, I think I have.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I see. I see. Yeah.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: This all comes out?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No. You may have to doctor this whole day. Oh, well that's okay. We just think that's stuff. I'm not a writer. I'm not a writer. Well, this is different. This is the way people, the way we talk, not the way we write. But then I guess Len always told his stories.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't write them down, did he?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No. No. No, he didn't write them down. He was a squawman . Len Henry was a squawman . He married an Indian woman. And they were called a squawman . And maybe that should be put in somehow. But he was quite a character. And there are a lot of kids down there that, good kids too, of the Henry kids. A lot of good kids. But he could come in and cross his leg and sit down and tell the most ridiculous, fabulous stories. And just keep you entertained for hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me another one of the stories that he told?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Okay. Now wait a minute.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, the Indians. The Indians was always after him, James.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He was always, though. You know, he drove, he always had his chest out, driving. That's not only that. He always had his chest out and be on a team street, driving eight horses, packing things back in the mountains. And he had a dog. And he said one time, he was driving those eight horses down around a damn trail. And he got the eight head of horses around the darn curve. And when he looked back, he said his dog was back there with a cramp in him. He couldn't get around. Crap in him. He couldn't get around that curve.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That would just go on for hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he tell one about changing his mind when he was in the middle of crossing the creek or river or something like that?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And that time there was a rattlesnake on the other side and he just turned around and went back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would he tell that one?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, he'd tell us that he was going to cross a stream. And when he jumped across the stream, he jumped out there and he saw a great big rattlesnake coiled up. And he just turned around and went back.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No, I can't see.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I don't care if they go any more.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, yeah, things like we'd... Like one time he was riding his horse down the trail and the Indians got after him. And boy, they were just about to get him, he said. And I throw the Lariathe out across one of the rocks out there. He said and hooked it on. He said, and I heard the old horse and it just flung right across the cliff on the other side. And the Indians... He put a half pitcher out of the saddle horn.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Yeah.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: But he was quite a catch. The Indians didn't try to cross, I take it. The Indians, they didn't try to follow him across. Oh, no. They couldn't get across there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he tell the stories when he told them? Was he poker -faced?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Yeah. I think that Lyn Henry told those for many times, these stories for many times, that I actually think and I've talked to a good many more that's heard him. That he's told us for many times that he thought there was the truth. And my gosh, if you would question it, he'd get indignant. He'd ready to fight you if you would question any of his stories.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he know a lot of them? Did he have a lot of them to tell?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Hell, he could sit there for hours and tell you just one ridiculous story after another that couldn't be possible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people try to match lies with him?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Yeah, they did when we sent him to California. They tried to match him and he came back. I don't know if he got anything out of it. We just paid his fares so he could go down there and got him back home again.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That's part of my life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Lyn Henry do to make ends meet?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, they're the big family of them. And of course in those days, the Indians, they had allotment coming, you know, and they had a little money coming every month through the agency. All of them had a little land, you know, and they leased it to the white men and they had a little money coming every month, enough to keep them going. Some of them, quite a bit, had their land, enough leased to the white people in my age, as I recall.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They didn't live like people, didn't have to have the things that we have nowadays.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: This is like your wife perhaps came home.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, yeah, he would get full of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there was really no other characters there that there were stories that you know of?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, no. He was the storyteller, all right, but I don't know too many of his stories.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Did you tell him about the dog that they made?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He couldn't get around the curb and bend in the trail or the road. Put an eight -horse team around there and your dog got crapped on.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Put your chair up.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Maybe you can think of someone to help him out.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: The old -time stories, my father disapproved of a lot of them. He says that these stories they write, the pictures they show of the West were exaggerated. And he crossed the plains. He said they didn't endure a lot of the things that they picture. He kind of complained about it, you know, it was wrong to mislead people like that. I think that's true. I think it is.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: But that's something that Westerners, a lot of us know, I think. A lot of people don't know. You don't know whether it's true or not.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: But he didn't approve of it because he said the stories were all exaggerated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he think there was a real true story that was a good story to tell, though?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No, he didn't tell too many stories. They lost, crossing the plains, they lost a member of their family. And the hardest part of that, he said, was just burying a member of your family. It just out, you know, wiles like that going on without them. He said they didn't have that trouble. They had, I think it was two working bosses that died of.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: But it's going to be careful what you're saying. I've been having it on and off. Oh, don't let it. They're not repeating anything I say.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And he said that was the hard part of it. For them, they had to bury a member of their family right out there, wild, open spaces.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he come to?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Illinois came to McConaughey County, Washington, down around Dayton and Walla Walla.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he homestead there?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: His father homesteaded there.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And you've heard him tell how they came into Lewiston, just drove, but they frayed it from Walla Walla up to Lewiston. And a father was quite a teamster. He loved horses. And they frayed it from Walla Walla. And he said, Lewiston was just nothing but a bunch of grass. And I could get a box up, a hole where the city is, for a song and a dance. And that's the only way they had to get afraid in there.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, I'm not a very good storyteller. I don't know. You express yourself real well. I think her parents were more pioneer than I was. I left home in Pennsylvania when I was 16 years old as a green country kid that came out of Portland, Oregon. But her folks were regular old pioneers that came out here in this country.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I think they were. My goodness.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And you won't find very many people. I don't suppose it's any older to be able to go back any further than we are. She's 85 and I'm going to get up to her age in February the 15th. And then I get to take over and I'll be in charge then after the 15th of February. We're both 85 years old. But then she'll be ahead of you again before much longer. I'll have the pleasure of being General Manager from February until next November again. Well, tell me this. Was Len Henry... He was really well known for his stories, for his lies when you were around Lapwai . Oh, yes.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: We didn't... Lapwai  people didn't have much to do with that class. It just happened that you'd run into him someplace. But really, the white people didn't associate too much with the higher class of Indians. And the Indians had very little respect for a white person who'd marry an Indian.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And he was a squaw man.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He was supposed to be a white man and he lived with Indians, married an Indian, and they wouldn't raise quite a family of boys. All of those kids turned out of level to them. Yes, they did pretty well. You did. Those boys did pretty well. But they did it on their own. They didn't do it for many help from him because he just didn't have it.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Isn't that funny now?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He would become popular just because he wasn't in the mountain of things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'll tell you why. Because he's a big liar. And there are very few around. I don't know of one in this county of all the people that I know in the early days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I know one guy that used to like to tell lies, but his reputation was very local. I don't think many people knew about him. Just a few people around, right around where he grew up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Len Henry seems to be known as quite a character. And I don't know why. He didn't seem like quite a character to you. It just seemed like something you do want to just stay away from, you know.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He wasn't what you'd call boring. He'd come into the store there in Sweetwater. And just sat down. Of course, he'd listen if anybody listened to him. He'd just sit there and entertain you. But I didn't have time. He doesn't have time to listen to all of them. He came right in the blacksmith's shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do? Did he get harsh a shot?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He never did. I don't think he ever spent a dime with me. I don't think he had a dime spent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your blacksmith's shop was a place where the guys used to get together?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: These are stories that I told you that somebody else told me. I was thinking maybe it was a place where guys would congregate. Not around his place. No. It just seemed so that he had nothing else to do. And if anybody listened to him, he'd sit down at the store or any place, you know. He was in the shop there and I'd busy it. I didn't have any time to listen to him. But if somebody else would, he'd entertain him.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I never heard him talk. I've seen him and I thought, ooh. I could hear him like that. But I haven't heard him. He was pretty dirty. Well, you think of it as being pretty dirty. But the Indians, to me, were either very clean or very dirty. Exceptionally bright or very stupid. I taught there, just Indians one winter, just substituting and filling in for a government teacher. And the Indian children were either exceptionally bright or exceptionally stupid. They were non -mediated over passive children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder if there really were just like two classes.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I can't account for that. I don't really know.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Now, I know sisters who married and had families. And one of them, that slick -poo woman, Nancy Slick -Poo, was very clean. Her floors were just spotless. And her sister was pretty and so dirty. So I really don't account. I don't have a...

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Of course, I was always at a point with the Indian people. I liked them. I liked their history. Yeah, I did too. I just, excuse me, would have had that too. The Indians have always had my sympathy, always, and still have. I've lived in there 27 years from the time we first landed in there with the old blanket Indians and all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are the old blanket Indians? Well, they did more pans, were they?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They were the original old Indians. That was a lot of, a certain amount of land on the Nez Perce   Indian Reservation.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And their land was leased by the white people. And it was their money was handled by the agency there at Lapwai . And every month their rent money was prorated rather, and think up and get their money the first of each month. And they were a wonderful class of people. They were like the people of today's on Social Security. They knew they were going to get a check at the end of the month, and they spent it. And they were honest. They told you, I'll pay you next month. I never had one beat me out of an account. They were there and paid it. But you had the next generation then.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That then the Indian got up to the place, some of them, that they were classed. First class, second class, and third class. Well, your first class began to get the deed for the land. Well, the Indian never could cope with the white people. So the white people beat them on the lambs, I see it. But anyhow, your next generation then, that was all split up. There were two people, two kids, or maybe five, maybe ten. Until, good gosh, they didn't have anything to live on in the second round. So, I don't know if they blamed the Indian for a lot, but they kept my sympathy of that. They never was trained to work because their fathers didn't know how to work, didn't know how to do anything. When they say first class, second class, where did this come from, this idea of... The government. The government, after they got educated well enough and understood enough why they would give them a deed to this piece of property. And then often times that land would be bought by whites, then they'd lose it. They leased a lot of the white men, they leased a lot of their land. There's very few of the Indians anymore, it's got their land, we got a few up in here that's still holding on to their land and taking their lease money, and they played smart.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And the Indians have got my sympathy and all of us have had them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'll tell you, quite a few of the old timers in Laytaw County, Deary, Troy, and the country through there, feel very much that the Indians not only had a raw deal, but that they took better care of the land than the white people do. I found that expressed often by old timers, and you know, just like you're saying your father didn't like the way he thought it was portrayed, well, you know, the pioneers are supposed to not like Indians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that's not exactly true from what I find, it's not true at all. Papa asked one time, he said, I'll wait, let me see if I can remember.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Somebody said, well, and what about this property, is it safe?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Now, I don't know whether it was horses and wagons or what it was. And the Indians said, no white men, miles, miles, so it was safe. But the father said, he'd tell the story, he said, was this property, whatever it was, wagons, of course, safe here.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No white men, miles and miles, it was safe.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Dad was quite a, quite fond of them, he kind of liked them. When we came there to that way, they were, as Lou says, were just wearing their blankets. I think they wore long johns, and then their blanket and the big black hat with a feather in it. And they dressed that way for a long time. I don't know when they made the change to white men's clothes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever get much of an idea of how the Indians there felt about the Joseph and the retreat in the war in 1877?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I think they were very proud of Joseph. No, I don't think we remember. But I have never, the friends we have who had the most information have passed away this recently. They have so much information about. I think the Indians as a whole were very proud of Joseph.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because you probably heard and didn't know that at the time there was a pretty strong split between the Indians on the reservation and the Dreamers, they called them, like Joseph and those others that didn't want to go.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, I never heard them express themselves too much in regard to, except that he was quite a hero to the Indians as far as we knew them.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Now I walk and look back, now that I get older, I look back over life. And we have progressed so fast, we have progressed to the point, as I see it, that the engineers are just the way behind us, saying. And I have always made the wrong mark when I hear some people just meeting the Indians, and they did this and that and that and that. I just kind of get right behind the ears. Because I feel this way, if the Indians were way back there in Columbus time, when they first come over, they had the same equipment and guns and things that the white man had when they came here. There wouldn't be any white men here today. But just imagine if you think about, all they had was just a spear and a knife to fight against. And I began to train them to work. We're just getting to that place right now, where the young kids going to a high school is realizing that they're going to have to work to get this dollar. You have to have that to buy things. So they're learning right now that to live more or less like the white man. Too much so.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, when the white man came in, you're not taping this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Come on, don't let it bother you.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, maybe I should stop it.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That's only our opinion and I.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That's what started the young people. Young people, I had some wonderful friends there among the young people. When the white man came in with brand new cars and the liquor. The ruined one couple that I just thought so much of, the finest young people. And they came in with liquor and sold them a car and they wound up in, oh, jail. What have you. Good delugeer. Fine young couple. It's the, I've read about the real efforts to keep liquor off the reservation. That was about one of the most serious and usual problems that they had on the reservation.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And in court cases, well, half of them are more.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They can handle liquor. Indians cannot handle liquor. And it just ruined some of my very best friends among those young people. I just thought so much of those young people there. I had the post office and I worked in the post office. Those young fellows, they come back from school back east, you know, and what they would just help you, they'd do it. They'd do it in the world for you. They like you, they just do anything in the world for you.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Yeah. Our next person did especially, I think, are a wonderful tribe. And I think now they're, that they're making progress, they're there at Lapwai .

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Have you been down in there and know anything about the area?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, yes. I think they're making progress. They're now training them to live like white people. They have built, I don't know how many homes right around in Lapwai . Oh, they're still building. And they didn't build them for them. They had, oh, they had what we'd call them, engineers or, but anyhow, they had help. And it was up to the engine to help to build this home. They put them right in on the project. We're going to build this house, but you're going to have to have it. And we went down there one scene and see the whole darn family maybe up on the roof here, doing that, putting the roof on and so on. Well, now they've never been able to learn that before because their forefathers didn't do such things. But I think they're living now more like white people, especially on top of the next person. And the first tribe down there that they're training, the kids going to our school and changing them to the place that they know they've got to work somehow to make the dollar.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They never were trained to use their land to, you'd take a lot of them down there, still got maybe two and a half or five or six acres. Well, you give that to a Chinaman or a Jap, they'd make a living, but the Indian has never been trained to do that. So it's coming up there, as I see it.

speaker: Sam Schrager

She said, you never saw anything so funny in your life. She said, well, I think you'd better go back to your handkerchief. You look more at home in your handkerchief. And they carried, the Indian women carried their money tied up in a red handkerchief tied to their belt. Well, if they'd buy three yards of material for a dress, they'd pay for that. But they'd turn around this way, get their money out, and they'd pay a dollar and a half for their worth. Then they'd buy something else, maybe a package of beans and munchke. Oh, put them to a nox quarter. So they would turn their back again on time. They'd pay for each thing separately. Each thing separately. Then they could keep track of it better that way.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: It was a matter we started there in Lapwai . It was in the horse age then. See, we didn't have any automobiles until 1915. It was the first automobile that came into Lapwai . So we were there and started doing our business in 13. Really in the horse age. So our business was shoeing horses in the harvest time when they hauled all the wheat and everything up over the hills there and bring it down to Lapwai . It was our business was to shoe horses and repair the wagon, set the tires, and, well, in a repair work that might be needed on equipment that they used those days.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Then in about, oh, along 1915, 1916 in there, things begin to change from the horse age into the megadized machinery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you pretty aware of that at the time, that there was a change underway?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Yes, yes, I was aware of it. I was among the first, and I'm not saying this to stick my chest out so I can't speak a word, but I was among the first that put in a settling welding. Among the first when settling was first developed. And they tried to sell it to a blacksmith because he's supposed to know a little something about heat, you know, so on. And I was among the first when the arc welding came in. We progressed as they went on.

speaker: Sam Schrager

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith

Just about time after it was running good, I'd make a whole armful a lot of times down the spot on this faulting. They were running out of hooks, lost them. So I had a point in my business.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a hitch did you design actually?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, it was a dream that I had.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And all hitches that are made even today still got a solid tongue. And I designed one that had a sector gear in it, which was set just like the front of an automobile wheel. But every move the tractor made where the tongue would work with it, like on a hillside or turning on a corner, instead of dragging the hitch around in the machinery, why the wheel is turned and we wheeled around the corner. And on a steep hillside, the tractor is always working uphill. Well, my tongue pulled the wheels off of the wheels. So those were my features. A piece of equipment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a pretty good blacksmith, eh?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Sounds to me like he was.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, he should have been. He took that training. A man that really knew the business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was who he was apprentice to back east?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: In Pennsylvania. I learned my trade from a Dutchman. In a little old shop on the four corners of the road, not in the city. Out in the road. And we did everything the hard way. We had no equipment, no power equipment. Everything was done by hand. There wasn't such things as power those days. The old bellas, if you know what a bellas is. Yeah, it's it. You got one out of the museum.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when did you start? When were you apprenticed?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I was 13 years old when they throw me in the blacksmith shop. I had a third degree of education. And what education I got after that, I got by experience.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many years was an apprenticeship for?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Three years. I went in and they throw me in the blacksmith shop on the 13th and I left home on the 16th. Three years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you do? Would you mostly watch and do the laboring jobs?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No, hell no. And through years of apprenticeship, I saw that I could shoe horses just as well as when I left home. They had to be a pretty good shoe, if they had put on more shoes than I could in a day. And I learned to twist iron around a little bit in the forge and so on. Oh yeah, three years of apprenticeship. You're right in the shop where you see up and you do it. Don't do that anymore. Not at all. But then blacksmithing is becoming a lost art anyway. Well, yes, to a certain extent.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: But many welders told me, oh, I'd give my left arm if I just knew a little bit about the forge work.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I was just going to ask if there were any experiences that you had at Lapwai  that really stand out to you when you remember these. Yes, hell yes. During the Depression, I burnt out. When things got so dark going on bad, I couldn't pay my, we couldn't pay our premium.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: After paying it how many years?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: You see, during that Depression, you couldn't collect anything.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Farmers could give you potatoes and wood and what have you, but money?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No. Excuse me, I'll tell you a little about this. So we lived through that, you see, that Depression.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: You just, there's no money. That isn't a beautiful thing just over this morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he burnt out when there was no, and you had no insurance?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, that's terrible. The whole thing, we had a family of seven.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Children?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No. My parents were with us, and we had three sons. And then, ourselves, and we started all over again. And that was in 30, let's see.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Twenty -three that we moved down there.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: What was between 23 and 30?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: 1930. It burned out.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: You know, I have to have my wife help me out a little bit, and bear me out on some of the things. I may get a little radical on that, but, yeah.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: During the Depression, then 1930, wasn't it?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, that's what I was telling him, that that fire must have been between 1925 and 1930, because we had moved down on the acreage.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, anyhow, the dates of it. It had been about 1930. That's right. But if you don't know what hard times in which you don't have with you, during that 30 it was really rough. It was rough. We never lived any better since or before, or since, I mean to say, because I could trade my work for stuff to eat all right. But that Depression was a blow to us. It got so that we just couldn't get money enough, we couldn't pay our premium. And it went along, jumped from one thing to another. I'll try to make this story short. But anyhow, a fellow called me from Genesee here. I'd come up and shoot his thud horse. And I hesitated because the darned old devil always hurt me every time I shot him. So I begged off and I said, no, I don't want to shoot him, but I don't want anybody else to shoot him. Well, I said, I can't come up there for the price that I've always come.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I'll come up and show him, I said, for $15. Well, $15, my God, would really something if I get this cash. He says, you come on up, I don't want anybody else to shoot him. So I got my older son, I told him about it, and he was pretty good help by that time. And I said, let's go up on Sunday morning and shoot that little devil, which we did. And we went on back home, and instead of me going up to the shop and putting my tools away like I usually did, my brother -in -law and sister -in -law, they went along down there and followed us all down. And then we had dinner and we played P. Knuckles up till dinner and so on. And after dinner, they beat the devil out of me, and after dinner I said, come on, go outdoors while we're cleaning up the dishes. You can't beat me on a full stomach. We got outside and the fish, the smoke was a roll out of town. I said, my God, there's a fire up town. And we took off up there and my shop was on fire from one end to another. So we were in pretty bad shape now, I want to tell you.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I think we had $12 .50 from the bank, wasn't it?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: $2 .50 in the pocket.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: But an Indian saved your books. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Yes, it was an Indian that broke through the front wind and got my daily and my ledger out. So, oh, it didn't amount to too much for the civil reason and damn nobody comes, maybe even them, because they couldn't. I left us pretty bad shape. I said, I got to make a story, a long story short.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, as I said, we got to make a long story short. The next day after the fire, we had a little old T -model, Ford, and she and I went up the Soldier Canyon Road where we grew up there with our 22 -and -shoot ground hogs and so forth. And we drove it up there and drove it into the fence in the evening and sat there and bowled like two kids. Now, if you want to know the truth, so after so long a time she said, this isn't going to get us anywhere. She said, oh, I know that. Let's begin to get down here and think what we're going to do. Well, in the meanwhile, the garage shifted during that pressure and they went broke, absolutely broke. And that building was vacant right next to me. And I saved the building by getting on top during that fire and another fella. And what little water we had, we sheared over the top and saved their building. So we went over the next morning, jumping things into the making a long story short. We always saw the lady that owned it. And she says, come in. She says, I was expecting you. And I asked her if she would rent her building. And of course, that was a garage and it had some tools and equipment in there. So she rent the building.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: With the first month, if you just put the windows in that the kids broke out and so on. And they were just covered with burlap and so on. You can have the first rent, first month rent free until you get on your beaches. I'll just rent you that for $1 ,000 a month.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Can you imagine that?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: So that started. Well, I could salvage quite a bit from a fire such as the animal and many of my tools that went through the fire. Once I get a forge, I can heat tweet them, you know, and put them back in shape, which I did. So it took quite a while to get started again. We made it in about 10 or 12 days. We've had some help. No one that ever came in to volunteer to give you a real lift with the exception of three fellas who did all kind of cement work. Moreland Brothers. You have three brothers of them.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And after I got in the shop in a few days, one of the first things I could see there, I needed a pair of horse, or saw horses, built to begin to make something. Pale and nothing. I just, I don't know, nothing came right because my tools were all gone. But while I was at that getting started, these three brothers backed up their truck at my back door there in the shop with a whole load of brick.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And one of them, he says, say, Lou, he says, where do you want that forge? And I said, what are you guys doing? What are you talking about? And he says, where do you want that forge?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And we came up here to put you up a forge. And I said, I haven't got any money and I can't pay you.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Who the hell ever said it in the hell of a month? Where do you want that forge?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And the three of them got in there and by the night they had the forge and everything built for them. And I believe the next day or so, some of the salesman came along. And that's where I appreciated, where I was really setting was the fact that my credit was still good. Herb Hardwair, salesman, came in. I don't know if there's anything that I needed now. I was just trying now to get organized against it. And I said, God, I don't know. I need so much stuff for the kid to buy everything. Now listen, he says, just before I left the house, I went to Herb Hardwair. He says, I guess, order anything that Messersmith wants and you sell it to him. And I said, okay, that takes him finally a nine -dollar order. A couple of bars of iron, a sack or two of coal. So I was all the way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like you bounced back pretty well.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, that's quite a story. You could lengthen enough. There's no money, though. I'm just thinking that's really something. But I was so anxious. The fifteen dollars that I got for showing that stud horse, I overlooked a little bit there. The fifteen dollars, I tell you, that was the biggest fifteen dollars I ever saw. But that's what happened then, that very day that I did that while I burned out. And I probably would have caught it had I gone to the shop like I should have. And put my tools away. I would have caught that fire before, because it really didn't get a good start for a long time in the afternoon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what caused it?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, yes, I can tell just exactly what caused it. That Saturday, this was on Saturday when they called me, and I was so anxious to make this fifteen dollars. And I knew just exactly the size shoes to make for the stud horse that I got busy and fit those shoes up. And I had done some acetylene welding that day, and it's an old wooden shop. And I never left the shop without taking the hose and wetting everything down, you know, manure and stuff underneath there. But this time I was more or less excited that it was going to make fifteen dollars. And I forgot to put that dab water on. So I evidently had sparks or something from my acetylene welding. I got down and I got the manure and stuff, and finally burned over to where they got air, and once it got that wet.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: It's gone. Yep.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That's quite a history on it now, isn't it?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That's quite a story.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Quite a story.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, it's not just a true story, that's the way it is. We've been awful lucky in our lives since. We're thankful. Every day we wake up and say we have so much to be thankful for. We live different, a lot of people.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That wouldn't take a lot for our experience in that way because... Oh, no, I wouldn't do it. I really like the Indian people. I really like them. I didn't care so much for old Aunt Henry, but the genuine Indian, as pretty as I liked them. They were interesting. Very fun though. When you say interesting, could you expand on it a little bit and what you mean by...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it about them that made them interesting to you?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, they were happy to me in their mode of living and their cooking. They cooked some very fine foods and their knowledge of nature, you know. The foods that they dug out of the earth, the berries, the way they could handle those things. It was interesting to me. Their beadwork, that was all by hand. They didn't have any patterns for anything.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: The patterns that they put on their baskets and all were all original. They didn't have patterns like we had for our crochet and our needlework. That was just natural power for them.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: What they could do with so little is what interested me. They got so much good out of nature, what nature provided for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever go to any of the ceremonies?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No, went to their churches. Yes, went to their Indian War dances. We often went to their war dances.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, we did go one time to their deal up there out of Craigmont.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Call that?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Call the Tutxinmepu. Every year they have a religious gathering. Cat meeting, they called it.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: It used to be Isle of Lord Nod's, Craigmont, and our time. They conduct at themselves.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They have a fine meeting, speaking and singing. They have good voices. We had a quartet there in that way that was real good. They'd meet there and sing. Gather them going home at night. Sometimes those fellows would sing all the way down the railroad track. It was a good quartet.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They played musical instruments too, didn't they?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Those younger fellows went to college.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They came home from college. It wasn't high classes. They were music like you hear on TV the day that they had their... David played.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Quite a number of those fellows played musical instruments, cornet and saxophones.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They were... They asked very little help of anybody. They still do quite a little bit of their corn husk work. They don't do so much beadwork, but they do make corn husk baskets. They do a lot for corn husk. But the Indian women still wore the same old clothing, those dresses. They had no patterns for anything. They just make their dresses just the same today, but they did then. Of course, the young people, but the older women make the same materials, same patterns.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I've heard that some of the young people are interested in the Indian ways of doing things there. So maybe the arts won't die.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I'm afraid they will. I'm afraid that they will. They say on Tuesdays, the Indian women still demonstrate its folding there, some of their work and how it's done. I hope that they do that again this summer because I want to take the family down there. One Indian woman I still know does that type of work. I want to see her and talk with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see them work when you were down there, or was that done usually just indoors?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, when you were living down there, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I watched them do their work. I hope that they will still be demonstrating its folding this summer. As you say, it will be a lost art. Those locals are not learning it.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: There's just a few of those older women left who do it. I want to be sure and go down there.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I really think that they're working on that pretty strong to try to bring that back. Kind of interesting here just to...

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: when did I send that pair of gloves last week and go to my son?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: A little short story here that I'll tell you about. We had a pair of gauntlet gloves here. Well, to go back to the original when I was running the shop there at Lapwai, there was a young Indian who came into the shop one afternoon late, end of the week, and this was nothing unusual.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: He said, say, the message to me that says, would you loan me two and a half?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I said, hell no. I said, I have no bank. Something like that is about the way I told him probably. Well, he said, I'll leave a little security here. I need two and a half, pretty bad this weekend. And so on. He showed me a pair of gauntlets and gauntlets. That was the most beautiful piece of beadwork you ever saw. He said, I'll leave them to you. I'll get back Saturday and pick them up. I'll give you money back. Okay, I gave him two and a half and I'll throw the gauntlets into the little office I had there and the desk I had. I lived up and threw it in there. And those darn gauntlets were in there, I guess, six months. And he never did come back to pick them up. Beautiful piece of work. Well, let me add an Indian lady there that is, well, we were just well acquainted with, and we still don't know everything. Mrs.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Black Eagle, you say, or?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No, I don't think it was. But anyhow, there was an Indian lady there that actually made buckskin gloves. And she looked at them, oh, she said, that's the most beautiful work I've ever seen. It was two beadwork.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: She said, let me make you a pair of gloves for him, okay?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: So she measured up my fingers and she made me a pair of gloves.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, they were so beautiful. And I had just left home not too long, and I had a sister back in Pennsylvania, and I thought, no, that would really be something. I had to send her. And I said, plus three beaded moccasins wasn't for her three daughters. Not realizing, you know, that people back there, that was just no value at all, I say. So, all right, we'll jump from that.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: But I said, those gloves.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, this summer, one of the daughters of the conversation somehow, I don't know how it came about, but she brought up this subject about the gloves that we sent back there to my sister, which is back there in 19, about, what would you say, 20 or longer. Well, that date don't matter anyhow. But as, going back then, she, finally, the daughter says, she says, I don't remember a thing about them, not a thing. And she didn't even remember the moccasins that I sent three girls. She was one of them. She was the youngest in the family. And after so long a time, well, she said, that pair of gloves means so much to your uncle Lou.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I begged them. I just begged them.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: She says, if they are still there, nobody cares for that. That's your uncle Lou, that you would like to have them. She said, that when I get back home, she says, oh, sure, look around. Of course, my sister's dead now, say, it was at that time, and for many years she's been dead. And sure enough, after she's back there a month or so, I guess one ear -cleaver box wasn't dead. With that pair of gloves in there.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: And she had to insure them, I guess, for $2 .50, wasn't it?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Because we had made such a damn fuss about them, say.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: So we have our youngest son, he's kind of gathering that kind of stuff together. Just a couple of days ago, we sent him to us, say, send him to California to us. Oh, yes, so many things are beautiful. And I insured those things for $250 because my oldest son, he saw some in an antique shop somewhere up around Coeur d 'Alene. And when he looked at him, he says, my God, he says, they're pretty, you're the one that I look, they had a price on $1 ,500.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That's all.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: That's that story. Too bad I don't have them here to show you, but just a few days ago, we sent them.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Anything I gave him, my beaded bag.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Yeah, he's collecting those kind of things.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I was given everything away because Dick really went to school there and the Indian boy played the piano for him. He played trumpet and the Indian boy played the piano. He was very fond of him. And I thought that it would mean more to him than it would anyone else in the family. We've tried to give him most everything we have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it getting so I'm keeping you from dinner?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: It's getting on towards life here. Well, I'm not very much in need for dinner. I visited my sister with the hospital and how in the world anybody can live. So she had some hoses in her nose, these in her wrists, and a hose attached to her tummy. I wish I hadn't gone. She looked terrible. The doctor said she's fine. Just get her along. Major surgery at the school. The boys were athletic, the Indian boys were, and they played basketball, were champions all the way through. But they weren't allowed to take any part in the school affairs. They would think a hard girl wouldn't be allowed to dance with one of those boys. They'd bait them or have anything to do with them at all. So this one Indian boy who was a neighbor would come in our home and play the piano for our boys. The boys all had instruments and played at school. And this Indian boy was quite a musician, and he sang. And our oldest son and his boy were from quite a duet and sang for our school affairs. But they didn't play together and work together like they do today. They weren't acting at schoolwork.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell your son's Indian stories when they were young? Bedtime stories and that sort of thing?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Not too much. Well, he didn't have to because our oldest kid was born right up to the old Indian. Born and raised right there among them. They never tell a name.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: The little Indian boy came in when they said he was out in the yard playing with the boys. He came and said, oh no, I happened to be outside. And I heard this little Indian boy say, Mrs. Messersmith, Dick, he cheat. Marvels. I remember that. I was so big about that move down there. And this little fella said, this measurement, Dick, he cheat.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Marvels. And you can't cope with white man at all. But the boys played with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean, white man is where he's going to get away with whatever he wants to? Is that the way? You just can't deal with what he does?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, I'd say up to date, the white man is just advanced a little further, I guess, in the Indian. And the white man takes advantage of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear of any of the Indian stories like Coyote?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I got all of that.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: I have all of that in this little book. You're welcome to read it. I was just wondering if you actually heard those stories when you were down there. If the Indians ever told stories about Coyote and that sort of thing. That story is in Mrs. Crawford's book, all those stories that they told. But my association with them were in the schoolwork or in the church work. And those stories were not told.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They didn't have dances around there like they had in some of the pioneer places where people just got together and had dances like at night in somebody's house or that sort of thing.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Or men, we didn't have them.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No, wait a minute. On New Year's night, the Indians, the U .S. We had their dance.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They had their dance at that thing. They never mixed at all at that time.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: The old Fennerson building on New Year's night. They had their regular war dances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you had regular dancing? You and folks who had dances get together, the white people would? With the Indians?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: No, by themselves. Sure. We had our dances and skating rink there at one time. But the Indians never came to the skating rink because I loved it.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Well, an Indian woman said to me one time, she says, Doesn't it make you jealous to see your husband skate with all these pretty girls?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, yeah. The Indians had their dances on New Year's. But it was strictly war dance.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: It was quite an event. Now they dance our style. Yeah, now they're dancing. They had their war dances too, but they called them heathens. The mission people called them heathens. They didn't like these war dances and these stick games. They said those are the heathens.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the religious Indians didn't take part in those then?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, no. Oh, no.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: They called them heathens.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there was sort of a split between those that did?

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Oh, yes. The heathens and the...oh, yes, definitely.

speaker: Lewis Messersmith or Hazel Messersmith
words: Yeah.
